data.title: Medtide Offering 16,800,000 Shrs at Maximum Price of HK$30.60 Each
Contract Drugmaker & Developer Medtide Launches HK Offering
Suning.com to Offload Four Carrefour China Supermarkets to Reduce Debt Burden
data.description:
Suning.com will offload four of its Carrefour China subsidiaries for a nominal value of 1 yuan each to Shanghai Jiafu Qishu Enterprise Service Partnership, according to a Friday filing with the Shenzhen bourse.

The sale involves Carrefour supermarkets in Ningbo, Hangzhou, Zhuzhou, and Shenyang, which face debt and financial performance issues, the filing said.

Suning.com will sell the loss-making supermarkets to focus on its home appliance business, according to the e-commerce platform operator.

Following the transaction, Suning forecasts its attributable profit to increase by 572 million yuan, the filing said.

Shares jumped 2% during morning trading on Friday.

data.title: Bitcoiners split on $94K or $114K for BTC’s next move as it trades sideways
data.description:
Crypto users are divided on whether Bitcoin will surge to an all-time high or again drop below the key $100,000 mark as the broader market is trading sideways, according to a recent poll on X.

Crypto pundits asked what’s next for Bitcoin (BTC) — $94,000 or $114,000 — split nearly evenly, with 50.2% predicting a drop to $94,000 and 49.8% expecting a rise to $114,000, according to the poll on Thursday by crypto analyst Matthew Hyland that gathered over 1,300 votes.

Sideways market takes a toll on crypto sentiment

Bitcoin is currently trading at $104,522, and a drop to $94,000 would mark a 10% decline, while a rise to $114,000 would mean an approximate 9% gain and a new all-time high, surpassing the previous high of $111,940 set on May 22, according to CoinMarketCap data.

Bitcoin hasn’t touched the $94,000 level since May 6, shortly before it broke back above $100,000 on May 8 for the first time in three months. 

It comes after Bitcoin briefly reclaimed $110,000 on June 11, leading to renewed hopes of new highs. However, escalating geopolitical tensions — triggered by Israel launching airstrikes on Iran last Thursday — caused Bitcoin to drop to $103,000.

The tensions have only been mounting since, with US President Donald Trump calling for Iran’s “unconditional surrender” and threatening Iran’s supreme leader, Ayatollah Ali Khamenei.

The ongoing sideways price action has dampened overall crypto market sentiment. In its June 20 update, the Crypto Fear & Greed Index flipped from “Greed” back to “Neutral,” posting a 54 out of 100 score.

Broader financial markets are also sideways

The S&P 500 is also trading flat, and is down 0.48% over the past five trading days to June 18, according to Google Finance data. 

Meanwhile, shares in Michael Saylor’s Strategy (MSTR) have dropped 2.43% over the same period and 10.74% over the past 30 days.

However, US-based spot Bitcoin exchange-traded funds continue to see inflows. The funds recorded $388.3 million in inflows on Wednesday to make it eight consecutive days of fresh capital.

Bitcoiners unsure whether bear markets are still a thing

Saylor said on June 10 that the bear market will not return for Bitcoin, but other analysts are not so sure. Crypto trader Rekt Capital recently said, “It’s very likely one will occur after this Bull Market.”

Some crypto analysts are placing bets that Bitcoin will be trading within the $130,000–$135,000 area in Q3.

BitMEX co-founder Arthur Hayes has firm hopes for Bitcoin to reach $250,000 by the end of this year.

This article does not contain investment advice or recommendations. Every investment and trading move involves risk, and readers should conduct their own research when making a decision.

data.title: BrainAurora Medical CEO Resigns
data.description:
Wang Xiaoyi resigned as chief executive officer and chief research officer of BrainAurora Medical Technology , effective June 19, a Friday filing to the Hong Kong bourse said.

Wang cited deteriorating health due to depression linked to the company's listing process as the reason for his departure, according to the filing.

Cai Longjun, currently chief technology officer and chief operating officer, has been appointed to act as CEO and CRO with immediate effect.

He will also assume legal and managerial responsibilities across BrainAurora's subsidiaries previously held by Wang.

data.title: LG HelloVision Launches No-Contract Internet Plans for 2030 Generation
data.description:
LG HelloVision launched its Yozome Plan Internet Promotion on Friday, offering no-contract internet plans for users aged 18-39 at 10,000 won to 20,000 won per month.

The plans, aimed at short-term residents, single-person households, office workers, and foreigners, include 160 Mbps internet at 19,800 won and internet with Wi-Fi at 22,000 won, LG HelloVision said in a Friday release.

The company is also offering up to 20,000 won in monthly discounts with affiliated cards.

Shares of LG HelloVision rose nearly 1% in recent trade on Friday.

data.title: Dalian iron ore set for weekly gains on resilient China demand
data.description:
Prices of Dalian iron ore futures rose on Friday and were poised for modest weekly gains on resilient demand for the steelmaking ingredient in top consumer China, outweighing a persistent slump in China's property market.

The most-traded September iron ore contract on China's Dalian Commodity Exchange (DCE) was up 1.36% at 706 yuan ($98.31) a metric ton, as of 0245 GMT.

The contract has gained 0.57% so far this week.

The benchmark July iron ore (SZZFN5) on the Singapore Exchange traded 1.42% higher at $94 a ton, but has eased 0.22% so far for the week.

The operating rate of blast furnaces in China edged higher in the week ended June 20 to reach 83.82%, up some 0.4% from the previous week, according to data from consultancy Mysteel.

Mysteel data also showed how hot metal output, a gauge of iron ore demand, inched up 0.24% week-on-week to 2.422 million tons, as of June 20.

"While demand is resilient now, the market's main focus is whether off-season demand will continue to be priced into market trading," said broker Galaxy Futures.

Spring is typically the peak season for construction in China ahead of the rainy season from June.

"Meaningful growth in steel and iron ore demand is unlikely until new construction activity lifts," said ANZ analysts.

China's new home prices fell in May, extending a two-year-long stagnation, official data showed on Monday.

Demand for new homes in China is likely to remain substantially below the market's 2017 peak over the next few years, Goldman Sachs said late on Monday, in a projection suggesting that the world's second-biggest economy faces a long property slump.

Other steelmaking ingredients on the DCE gained ground, with coking coal NYMEX:ACT1! and coke (DCJcv1) up 2.23% and 2.05%, respectively.

Most steel benchmarks on the Shanghai Futures Exchange strengthened. Rebar rose 0.57%, hot-rolled coil and wire rod (SWRcv1) both gained around 0.7%, while stainless steel shed 0.32%.

($1 = 7.1812 Chinese yuan)

data.title: Salesforce Not Taking Survival for Granted in AI Era, Top Strategist Says
data.description:
By Kimberley Kao

Salesforce is a giant in its field, but Peter Schwartz, one of its top strategists, isn't taking survival for granted in the era of artificial intelligence.

The unprecedented pace of change brought by AI is forcing the software maker to reinvent itself, or risk being put out of business, said Schwartz, chief futures officer of Salesforce.

"The magnitude of change is so great that this is the moment when great companies die and great companies are born," he said in a recent interview. "You make the wrong acquisition, the wrong product choice, the wrong set of standards and you're out of business."

Schwartz, 78 years old, has spent decades working as a business strategist, and describes the AI wave as the biggest technological change of his lifetime.

It is one that poses a significant challenge for Salesforce as it plans its product pipeline.

"I honestly don't know what AI will be doing two years from now," Schwartz said to The Wall Street Journal on the sidelines of the annual ATxSummit in Singapore. "Things that we're working on today may be irrelevant a year from now and certainly will be 18 months from now."

The crux of Schwartz's job, he said, is scenario planning by thinking two steps ahead. Part of his role also includes advising on its acquisitions strategy, which is "one of our innovation mechanisms," Schwartz said.

One way Salesforce stays ahead of the game is through backing AI companies with capital, Schwartz said.

The company's venture-capital arm has so far invested around $6 billion across over 600 businesses, with about $1 billion committed to AI startups alone.

About 30% of Salesforce Ventures' investments are in businesses outside of the U.S.

The company has been pivoting to AI agents--systems that can perform tasks on behalf of users in addressing customer service, marketing and business procurement needs.

Recently, the San Francisco-based company closed a $8 billion deal for data-management firm Informatica as part of its efforts to put AI to work. Schwartz said the move gives Salesforce a vital capability in AI data organization.

Informatica was the "right deal at the right time" for Salesforce, said analyst Daniel Ives of Wedbush Securities, expecting the move to support the company's agentic AI strategy.

Schwartz, who has consulted on Hollywood films like "WarGames" and "Minority Report," games scenarios as he aims to future-proof the company he has worked at for nearly 15 years.

Major events like the pandemic and the rise of Chinese AI--led by startup DeepSeek--weren't hugely surprising to the tech veteran, but U.S. political developments caught him off guard.

"If you do multiple scenarios you're rarely surprised," he said.

Salesforce's lean decision-making structure is an asset during the current period of significant turbulence, Schwartz said.

"Fortunately, there's only a few of us who are really directly involved in the decision making and we have a very decisive CEO. So we can move fast when we need to," he added.

Operating in 92 cities across the world means Schwartz spends a fair amount of time trying to understand how different markets are developing.

He calls it "geographic strategy." One of the biggest decisions Salesforce made in this regard was opting not to do direct business in China.

"We can't say to [customers] honestly that if we had a data center in China that we were running that we could provide that security [in terms of data]," Schwartz said.

Salesforce's workaround was to license software to China's Alibaba to support its customers in the country.

"Jack Ma is an old friend," Schwartz said of the Chinese tech giant's founder. "We found a good partner in Alibaba."

Schwartz has no plans to retire from trying to anticipate the next curve ball.

"I work directly for our CEO, Mark Benioff...I work frankly because of him," Schwartz said. "He's a giant, both as a business and a moral leader. And if he says I need to stay, I stay."

Write to Kimberley Kao at kimberley.kao@wsj.com

data.title: SNG: Cluster2, Airbus to advance smart airport operations in Saudi Arabia
data.description:
Staff Writer

Saudi Arabia - Cluster2 Airports Company has signed a memorandum of understanding (MoU) with Airbus during the 2025 Paris Air Show, which aims to introduce advanced smart technologies led by the Airbus Agnet Turnaround platform—an innovative solution designed to enable real-time coordination of airport field operations, reported SPA.

By adopting this platform, Cluster2 seeks to streamline workflows, boost operational efficiency, and enhance passenger experience across its network of 22 international and domestic airports.

The MoU also supports broader goals of raising operational standards and enabling seamless collaboration among ground teams, security personnel, and control room operators.

data.title: RAAS Blood Products Completes Full Acquisition of Nanyue Biopharmaceutical
data.description:
Shanghai RAAS Blood Products finalized its 4.2-billion-yuan acquisition of Nanyue Biopharmaceutical, following regulatory approvals and industrial registration updates.

The deal includes a 50-million-yuan contingent payment if Nanyue Biopharmaceutical achieves 305 tons of plasma collection in 2025, according to a Friday filing with the Shenzhen bourse.

Shanghai RAAS Blood Products completed the appointment of directors, supervisors and senior management for Nanyue Biopharmaceutical, now a wholly-owned subsidiary.

data.title: Thailand's Exports Face Threats From U.S. Tariff Chaos — Market Talk
data.description: Thailand's exports face significant downside threat from U.S. tariff chaos, particularly in 2H, DBS senior economist Chua Han Teng says in a note. The 90-day reciprocal tariff pause spurred front-loading of orders and contributed to May's 18.4% growth in goods exports from a year earlier, he writes. However, the temporary boost would be followed by a significant payback through decelerating trade in 2H. "The outlook is complicated by heightened uncertainty surrounding negotiations between Thailand and the U.S. aimed at reducing U.S. tariffs from the 36% proposed on Liberation Day," Chua says. (amanda.lee@wsj.com)

data.title: Medtide Sees Net Proceeds of HK$411.2 Mln at Midpoint HK$28.40-HK$30.60 Price Range
Aussie Dollar Pressured by Middle East Conflict
data.description:
The Australian dollar stabilized above $0.648 on Friday, but remained under pressure as escalating Middle East tensions and the potential US involvement in the region weighed on sentiment.

Global markets are now bracing for a possible US decision within two weeks, as President Trump weighs a potential direct strike on Iran.

However, the China-proxy currency found some support after the People’s Bank of China left its Loan Prime Rates unchanged, helping sentiment around regional demand.

At home, the recent mixed Australian jobs report did little to alter expectations, with a July RBA rate cut still priced at a 75% probability.

Westpac expects the next move in August, citing the central bank’s preference for caution and the need to assess second-quarter inflation and global conditions.

Investors now turn to upcoming PMI figures for a clearer read on Australia’s economic momentum.

data.title: Bowen Coking Attributes Liquidity Challenges to Depressed Coal Markets, 'Unsustainable' Queensland Royalty Regime; Shares Hit All-Time Low
data.description:
Bowen Coking Coal said its earnings were under pressure and it was facing liquidity challenges due to the depressed metallurgical and thermal coal markets, combined with the "unsustainable" Queensland state progressive coal royalty regime, according to a Friday Australian bourse filing.

The company was engaged in discussions with multiple parties to secure funding to support ongoing operations. The firm warned that it may need to consider temporarily pausing part or all of its operations until markets return if it is unsuccessful in securing additional funding to keep operating the business.

It earlier announced a transition of the Burton mine complex to an owner-operator model under the direction and management of Bowen Coking Coal employees from July 1.

The firm said it is exploring a range of strategic and financial sources, including potential debt, equity, and hybrid solutions, to fund the Burton owner-operator transition plan and to provide immediate liquidity.

It said that it had already achieved full-year run-of-mine coal production and coal sales guidance for the fiscal year ended June 30. It reaffirmed its intention to achieve the high-end of run-of-mine coal production and coal sales guidance and the low-end of free-on-board cost guidance for the fiscal year.

The firm's shares plunged 44% in recent trading on Friday, earlier reaching an all-time low.

data.title: Vietnam, US hold negotiations on new trade deal, ministry says
data.description:
Vietnam and the United States held an online round of negotiations on a new trade deal on Thursday night, the Ministry of Industry and Trade said on Friday.

During the negotiations with U.S. Secretary of Commerce Howard Lutnick and Trade Representative Jamieson Greer, Vietnamese trade minister Nguyen Hong Dien asked the U.S. to review "reciprocal tariffs" and market access for Vietnam's key exports, the ministry said in a statement.

The Southeast Asian industrial hub has held several rounds of negotiations with the U.S. to strike a deal that would allow Vietnamese goods to avoid a 46% tariff rate, imposed largely as a result of its big trade surplus with Washington.

"The negotiation round was conducted in a candid, friendly and constructive manner," the ministry said.

Minister Dien also welcomed proposals from Lutnick and Greer during their talks, the ministry added without elaborating.

Dien said Vietnam wants to develop with the U.S. a set of practical and harmonious rules of origin of goods that is in line with global supply chains, according to the statement.

data.title: South Korea's Extra Budget May Prompt BOK to Raise 2025 Growth Forecast — Market Talk
data.description: The South Korean government's proposed second extra budget is likely to prompt the Bank of Korea to raise its 2025 growth forecast to 1.0% from 0.8% at its August policy meeting, Nomura analysts say in a note. Fiscal stimulus is likely to ease growth concerns and strengthen the BOK's hawkish stance in monetary policy, they say. Nomura now expects no further rate cuts from the BOK this year and forecasts one last quarter-percentage-point cut in February 2026 for its current easing cycle. That should bring the base rate to 2.25% in South Korea. (kwanwoo.jun@wsj.com)

data.title: Global Forex and Fixed Income Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering FX and Fixed Income. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0305 GMT - The South Korean government's proposed second extra budget is likely to prompt the Bank of Korea to raise its 2025 growth forecast to 1.0% from 0.8% at its August policy meeting, Nomura analysts say in a note. Fiscal stimulus is likely to ease growth concerns and strengthen the BOK's hawkish stance in monetary policy, they say. Nomura now expects no further rate cuts from the BOK this year and forecasts one last quarter-percentage-point cut in February 2026 for its current easing cycle. That should bring the base rate to 2.25% in South Korea. (kwanwoo.jun@wsj.com)

0243 GMT - Thailand's potential prolonged political turmoil would undermine business confidence, DBS senior economist Chua Han Teng writes in a note. The Thai economy is already grappling with external headwinds from the U.S. tariff roller coaster, he says. "Domestic political uncertainty due to the exit of Bhumjaithai Party from Thailand's ruling coalition government is clouding an already fragile economic landscape," he adds. Bhumjaithai Party, the second-largest in the Pheu Thai-led ruling coalition, withdrew from the alliance earlier this week amid a lack of trust in Thai Prime Minister Paetongtarn Shinawatra, Chua notes. There was a leaked phone call with former Cambodian leader Hun Sen in which Paetongtarn criticized the Thai army. Bhumjaithai Party's withdrawal leaves the ruling coalition with a slim majority and little room for further exits, Chua says. (amanda.lee@wsj.com)

0229 GMT - Malaysia's sales tax expansion, effective July 1, is expected to generate 10 billion ringgit annually and support fiscal reform, RHB Investment Bank analyst Alexander Chia says in a note. While sectors like consumer, property and REITs may see slight pressure, targeted exemptions and sustained demand should limit earnings risk, he says. Chia sees room for the government to boost aid to lower-income groups and prefers defensive, domestic-centric stocks amid near-term trade and geopolitical overhangs. Additional revenue from sales tax could be used to stimulate the domestic economy during this period, he adds. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

0156 GMT - Most Asian currencies are stronger against the dollar in early Asian trading. Traders are keeping an eye on escalating hostilities between Israel and Iran as well as potential U.S. strikes on the Islamic Republic. President Trump says he will decide on whether to strike Iran "within two weeks," according to the White House. "The U.S. dollar is still the reigning world reserve currency, but of late has not been the most effective hedge against volatility," says John Sidawi, senior portfolio manager for international fixed income at Federated Hermes. USD/KRW is 0.4% lower at 1,370.70. USD/MYR is down 0.2% at 4.25. USD/SGD is 0.2% lower at 1.28. (amanda.lee@wsj.com)

0048 GMT - Japanese government bonds are under selling pressure following signs of persistent domestic inflation. The government said earlier that Japan's consumer price index, excluding fresh food, rose 3.7% in May from a year earlier, compared with April's3.5% increase and expectations for a 3.6% rise. Persistently high inflation is keeping market expectations intact for the Bank of Japan rate increases down the road, despite the economic uncertainty stemming from U.S. tariffs and the Middle East conflict. Ten-year JGB futures are 0.11 yen lower at Y139.22. The 20-year JGB yield is up half a basis point at 2.365%. (kosaku.narioka@wsj.com; @kosakunarioka)

0036 GMT - While Australia's May employment data saw a fall in jobs growth, the data is unlikely to shift the dial for the Reserve Bank of Australia, which considers the job market to be relatively tight, says Westpac economist Ryan Wells. Westpac remains comfortable with its view that the RBA's next rate cut is most likely to occur in August, he adds. The RBA is cognizant of the downside risks to global and domestic growth, but has also made it clear it wants to adjust policy in a cautious and predictable manner. (james.glynn@wsj.com; @JamesGlynnWSJ)

0026 GMT - The Philippine central bank is expected to continue cutting rates this year, with inflation set to stay low, Capital Economics senior Asia economist Gareth Leather writes in a note. BSP's decision to cut its policy rate on Thursday came as no surprise. The dovish tone from the central bank's statement and press conference suggests further easing is likely, he adds. "The economy could certainly do with a boost," he says. Sequential 1Q GDP growth slowed sharply. Low inflation and falling interest rates will also offer some support to demand this year, he says. Capital Economics expects another 50 bp cut from the BSP by year-end.(amanda.lee@wsj.com)

0019 GMT - Japan's Nikkei Stock Average is up 0.2% at 38577.17 as gains in chip stocks help offset losses in real-estate and heavy-industry shares. Kioxia Holdings gains 5.6% and Advantest is 2.2% higher, while Mitsui Fudosan falls 1.1% and IHI is 2.2% lower. Broader market index Topix is down 0.1% at 2790.44. USD/JPY is at 145.26, little changed from 145.25 as of Thursday's Tokyo stock market close. Investors are focusing on any developments related to the Israel-Iran conflict and its implications for energy prices. (kosaku.narioka@wsj.com; @kosakunarioka)

0004 GMT - South Korea's fiscal stimulus could lift 2025 growth by 0.3 percentage point, Goldman Sachs economists Irene Choi and Goohoon Kwon write in a note. The government's latest second supplementary budget proposal that entails a KRW14.9 trillion net increase in expenditures could add 0.1 percentage point to headline GDP growth this year, on top of the 0.2 percentage point added by the first extra-budget package, they project. South Korea's economy has been forecast to grow 0.8% in 2025, compared with 2024's 2.0% growth, according to the latest forecasts from the Bank of Korea and state think tank Korea Development Institute, which have recently downgraded their 2025 growth outlooks. (kwanwoo.jun@wsj.com)

2342 GMT - Japanese stocks may trade rangebound as uncertainty continues over the Israel-Iran conflict and its implications for energy prices. Nikkei futures are flat at 38510 on the SGX. USD/JPY is at 145.30, compared with 145.25 as of Thursday's Tokyo stock market close. Investors are closely watching any developments in the Middle East. The Nikkei Stock Average fell 1.0% to 38488.34 on Thursday. (kosaku.narioka@wsj.com)

1510 GMT - Dollar rises as air strikes between Israel and Iran raise market concerns and drive up demand for safe-haven assets."Ongoing Israel-Iran missile attacks and the possibility of U.S. involvement have increased [market] worries," IG's Axel Rudolph says in a note. The dollar DXY index rises to 99.061 after it touched a high of 99.157 earlier in the day. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

1338 GMT - Optimism among Canadian small-business owners continues to rebound after hitting a tariff-fueled historic low in March. The closely watched Business Barometer, published by the Canadian Federation of Independent Business, indicates the confidence index rose to 47.3 in June from 40.1 in the prior month. The reading below 50 suggests a greater share of business owners expect weaker sales over the next 12 months. Still, the June reading is well above the 25.5 record low registered in March, or when trade uncertainty was at a peak. "Early signs point to small businesses getting used to tariffs and finding alternatives," says the CFIB's chief economist, Simon Gaudreault. (paul.vieira@wsj.com, @paulvieira)

data.title: Unisound AI Technology Sees Net Proceeds at HK$176.6 Million at Midpoint of Price Range
CICC, Haitong Among Managers for Unisound AI Technology Offering
Thai Banks Face Near-Term Challenges — Market Talk
data.description: Thailand banks' asset quality and profitability prospects this year could be hindered by near-term challenges, Fitch Ratings analysts write in commentary. The country's growth seems likely to continue underperforming regional peers over the next few years, constraining business opportunities for banks, they say. The Bank of Thailand started its rate cut cycle in 4Q 2024, which will lead to thinner margins for banks. Rate cuts are expected to be limited compared with the aggressive monetary easing during the pandemic, Fitch says. It expects narrower margins and weak growth prospects to have a modest effect on banks' 2025-2026 profitability. Fitch revises its 2025 outlook for the Thai banking sector to deteriorating from neutral. (amanda.lee@wsj.com)

data.title: China's Li Ning rises most in over 3 months as key shareholder buys shares
data.description:
** Shares of Chinese homegrown sportswear group Li Ning Co Ltd rise 4% to HK$15.6, on course for their biggest one-day gain since March 14

** Key shareholder Viva Goods said its unit had bought an aggregate 18.1 million Li Ning shares between January 10 and June 19 for a total of HK$275 million ($35.03 million)

** ViVa shares gain 1.5% to HK$0.345

** Hang Seng Consumption Index (.HSCGSI) adds 0.1%, Hang Seng Index rises 1%

** YTD, Li Ning stock down 6.1%

($1 = 7.8498 Hong Kong dollars)

data.title: Copper prices range-bound on geopolitical uncertainty
data.description:
The most-traded copper contract prices on the Shanghai Futures Exchange and London Metals Exchange were range-bound on Friday, as market sentiment was mixed amid ongoing uncertainty over the Israel-Iran conflict.

Israel bombed nuclear targets in Iran on Thursday and Iran fired missiles and drones at Israel after hitting an Israeli hospital overnight, with U.S. President Donald Trump saying he will make a decision within the next two weeks on whether to get involved on Israel's side.

"A few issues have been stirring the metals market sentiment, and investors are divided on how to assess these, most importantly the Middle East, but also the possible U.S. interest rate cuts, and China's lower consumption, just to name a few," said a Shanghai-based metals analyst at a futures firm.

The most-traded copper contract on the Shanghai Futures Exchange eased 0.1% to 78,400 yuan ($10,907.07) per metric ton as of 0103GMT, up 0.04% on the week, while three-month copper on the London Metal Exchange gained 0.3% to $9,641 a ton and was little changed on the week.

China's refined copper output in May gained 13.6% on the year to 1.25 million metric tons, data on Wednesday showed, in line with April's output, while the country's demand for metals such as copper and aluminium has been muted by summer seasonal weakness.

LME tin gained 0.5% to $32,180, aluminium went up 0.4% to $2,531, and zinc inched up 0.2% to $2,644.5.

SHFE tin fell 0.9% to 261,390 yuan a ton, aluminium eased 0.2% to 20,555 yuan, while lead gained 0.2% to 16,915 yuan, and zinc inched up 0.1% to 21,925 yuan.

For the top stories in metals and other news, click

or

DATA/EVENTS (GMT)

0600 UK Retail Sales MM, Ex-Fuel MM, YY May

0645 France Business Climate Mfg, Overall Jun

1230 US Philly Fed Business Indx Jun

1400 EU Consumer Confid. Flash Jun

($1 = 7.1880 Chinese yuan)

data.title: Dow Jones Top Markets Headlines at 11 PM ET: Oil Prices Rally, Stock Futures Fall in Holiday-Thinned Trading | Japan's ...
data.description:
Oil Prices Rally, Stock Futures Fall in Holiday-Thinned Trading

U.S. index futures fell Thursday, as conflict in the Middle East saw global oil prices rally further.

----

Japan's Core Inflation Picks Up in May

Persistent price rises further complicate the Bank of Japan's policy steering amid tariff-related uncertainty.

----

Surging Silver Prices Prompt Americans to Empty Jewelry Boxes and Coin Jars

Strong industrial demand brings the precious metal's highest price levels in more than a decade.

----

Canada Reveals Steps to Protect Steel Sector Under Duress From Trump Tariffs

Prime Minister Carney said the policy changes address 'destabilization' of metals sector due to U.S. trade policy.

----

Trump Will Decide Within 2 Weeks Whether to Intervene in Israel-Iran War

Trump kept the world guessing on whether the U.S. would join Israel in attacking Iran.

----

Oil Prices Are Jumping. What History Says Happens When Conflicts Threaten Supply.

For the past 40 years, the biggest increases in oil prices have all been driven by demand, not supply.

----

Bank of England Mirrors Fed and Stands Pat

Central banks are facing a new challenge as tensions between Israel and Iran threaten to push energy prices and inflation higher.

----

Bitcoin Price Rises. Watch the Dollar for Clues on Where Cryptos Goes Next.

The world's largest cryptocurrency doesn't seem to be getting much of a boost from rising geopolitical uncertainty.

----

Foreign Investment Faces Third Year of Decline, UN Warns

Overseas investment by businesses around the world is at risk of falling for a third straight year as rising tariffs and geopolitical tensions freeze big decisions about where to locate factories, the UN warned.

----

China's Real Estate Recovery Is Mixed. These Cities Are Bouncing Back.

Luxury homes in Shanghai are selling but prices are still falling in smaller cities.

----

ECB's Next Move Most Likely to Be a Rate Cut, Villeroy Says

Unless the eurozone faces a major new shock such as an escalation of the military conflict in the Middle East, the ECB is more likely to lower its key interest rate over the next six months than raise it, the head of the Bank of France said Thursday.

----

Swiss Central Bank Cuts Rate to Deter Search for Safe Haven

Switzerland cut rates to zero to rein in the rapidly appreciating franc, which has acted as a safe haven for investors given raised concerns over U.S. trade policy and Middle East tensions.

----

The Fed Waits for the Dust to Settle on Tariffs

Fed Chair Jerome Powell is engaged in a balancing act: projecting confidence while admitting "we don't know" what comes next.

----

How 'gold fatigue' has helped drive platinum prices up 44% this year

Platinum has far outpaced its metals peers for the month and year to date as supplies of the commodity are expected to fall short of demand for a third straight year. But that's not the only reason for platinum's stellar price performance.

----

Foreign Investors Sold $41 Billion in Treasuries After Trump Unveiled Tariffs

In April, foreigners sold a net $40.8 billion of U.S. notes and bonds that had more than one year to maturity.

data.title: Dow Jones Top Company Headlines at 11 PM ET: Trump Delays TikTok Ban for a Third Time | Home ...
data.description:
Trump Delays TikTok Ban for a Third Time

The president gave the Chinese-controlled video app another reprieve from a 2024 bipartisan law requiring its sale or closure.

----

Home Depot Bid Kicks Off Battle for $5 Billion Building-Products Company

Retailing giant submitted a bid for distributor GMS, which already received an unsolicited offer from serial dealmaker Brad Jacobs this week.

----

SpaceX's Starship Explodes During Ground Test

The setback was the latest in what has become an increasingly important bet for Elon Musk.

----

The Real Message Andy Jassy Is Sending to Employees on AI

Amazon's delivery workforce is safe for now, but its office staffers need to get on the artificial-intelligence bandwagon.

----

OpenAI Changes Price Structure for Business Version of ChatGPT

OpenAI is changing how it sells the business version of its ChatGPT chatbot, amid increasingly heated competition.

----

Microsoft Plans to Cut Thousands More Employees

A new round of workforce cuts at the Redmond, Wash.-based tech giant comes as the company makes a costly push into AI.

----

Canada Antitrust Watchdog Calls For Easing of Foreign-Ownership Limits in Airline Sector

The country's two major airlines, Air Canada and WestJet, account for about half to three-quarters of domestic passenger traffic, the bureau said.

----

EU Court Advisor Backs Google's $4.7 Billion Android Fine

A top adviser to the EU's highest court backed the penalty imposed by EU antitrust regulators, in a blow to Google's yearslong battle against the fine.

----

Switzerland's New Capital Rules Will Be Feasible for UBS, Central Bank Says

The required capital increase will be achievable for the banking giant and shouldn't excessively reduce its shareholder distributions, the Swiss National Bank said.

----

Mars Misses EU Deadline for Kellanova's $30 Billion Deal Remedies

The European Commission had not received by Wednesday's deadline any remedies from Mars for its deal to take over the Pringles maker, which it agreed to buy last August.

----

Rio Tinto Agrees to Settle Mongolia Copper Mine Lawsuit

The company agreed to pay $138.75 million to resolve a class-action lawsuit that alleged the company concealed problems during the expansion of a giant copper mine in Mongolia.

----

Bank of Montreal to Buy Burgundy Asset Management

The $456.4 million acquisition expands the bank's wealth management segment as well as strengthen its offering in the Canadian Investment Counsel space, the companies said.

----

Caris Life Sciences Jumps in Debut, Latest Win for Healthcare IPOs

Cancer-testing company raises $494 million in initial public offering.

----

Boeing 787 Crash: What We Know and What Comes Next

Investigations are focused on the engines and why the plane lost lift shortly after takeoff.

data.title: Slow rate hikes could cause wage-price spiral, BOJ paper says
data.description:
Key points:
  • Pass-through of material costs more moderate in Japan than Europe
  • Second-round effects moderate but sustained in Japan, Europe
  • Slow rate hike could be enhancing second-round effects
  • Paper comes amid heightened BOJ focus on creeping inflation

Hiking interest rates only gradually as raw material costs rise could heighten the risk of an upward spiral in wages and consumer prices, the Bank of Japan said in a research paper released this week.

The paper's publication on Thursday comes as the central bank faces an increasingly complicated policy environment, with inflation at a more than two-year high and U.S. tariffs fanning economic uncertainty.

While the staff papers do not represent the BOJ's official view on monetary policy, they provide hints on key topics of attention within the central bank in setting interest rates.

The BOJ staff paper, using data from 2002 to 2024, analysed trends in Japan and Europe - which both rely heavily on imported commodities - to study the extent to which rising material costs led to second-round effects on inflation.

In Japan, the pass-through of prices from rising raw materials was more moderate than in Europe, the paper said.

The second-round effects were moderate but sustained in both Japan and Europe, it said.

"Both in Japan and Europe, the initial effects of high raw material costs were the main cause of inflationary trends since 2020. The second-round effects may have heightened the sustainability of price rises," the paper said.

Central banks typically raise interest rates to avoid second-round effects on inflation, or a state in which initial price shocks like higher energy costs trigger a spiral of rising wages and inflation that could lead to a broad-based, persistent inflationary environment.

A closer look at Japan's data suggested the BOJ's slow pace of interest rate hikes could be enhancing the second-round effects on inflation, the paper said.

Structural changes in Japan's labour market could also be making wages less rigid - or more likely to move flexibly reflecting a tight job market - and enhancing the second-round effects on inflation than in the past, the paper said.

The analysis comes amid heightening attention within the BOJ board on how persistent rises in food and raw material costs could affect underlying inflation, and households' perception of future price moves.

While uncertainty over U.S. tariff policy has put the BOJ on hold in raising interest rates, Governor Kazuo Ueda has signaled the bank's resolve to keep pushing up borrowing costs if Japan stays on course to durably hit the bank's 2% inflation target.

Japan's core inflation hit a more than two-year high in May and exceeded the central bank's 2% target for well over three years, keeping it under pressure to resume rate hikes despite economic headwinds from U.S. tariffs.

data.title: Changchun High-Tech Gets China Approval for Elderly Flu Vaccine Clinical Trial
data.description:
Changchun High-Tech Industry subsidiary BCHT received the approval of China's drug administrator to begin clinical trials for its influenza split virus vaccine (BK-01 adjuvant).

The vaccine, intended for people aged 60 and above, combines traditional split virus technology with BCHT's proprietary adjuvant to enhance immune response, according to a Friday filing with the Shenzhen bourse.

Shares of the company were down nearly 1% in recent trade.
